data.title: Nike snaps six straight sessions of gains
data.description:
Nike (NKE) shares snapped six straight sessions of gains, as the stock closed 2.4% lower at $46.21 on Friday.

The sportswear giant gained about 7% in the preceding six sessions. Overall, the stock has fallen nearly 27% so far this year, compared to the over 10% rise in the broader S&P 500 Index.

Nike is up nearly 5% over the past one month. The stock closed 3% higher on Thursday.

The Beaverton, Oregon-based company has been facing pressure on many fronts. In April, investors reacted negatively to Nike’s forward guidance that predicted a 2% to 4% sales decline in fiscal Q4 and continued weakness in the critical China market, leading to the stock falling 15% following the release of its Q3 earnings report.

Earlier in the month, consumers of Nike also took the athleisure and footwear company to court, filing a proposed class action lawsuit over its failure to refund the tariff-related costs that it passed on to consumers through higher prices.

Nike has disclosed that Trump’s tariffs forced it to pay roughly $1B as levies on imports. Consumers argued that, as a result, the company increased the prices of some footwear and apparel by $5 - $10 and $2 - $10, respectively.

Looking at Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating, Nike has a Hold rating with a score of 2.77 out of 5. The company received a B in the prospect of profitability, while it got a D- in the growth and momentum factor.

Turning to the Wall Street community, 16 analysts gave Nike a Buy and above, 20 analysts have given the stock a Hold recommendation, and two recommended Sell or lower.

Seeking Alpha analysts are cautious and see the stock as a Hold.

“NKE faces diminished leverage with wholesalers and younger consumers, with new entrants like ONON and DECK capturing share and outperforming in wholesale growth,” said a recent Seeking Alpha analysis by RiverBoat Investing. It added that management's lack of long-term guidance, ongoing gross margin headwinds, and reliance on tariff explanations create further uncertainty during the company’s turnaround.

Seeking Alpha analyst Thomas Shields also believes Nike has a tough road ahead and that investors may benefit from staying away from this company in the near term.

data.title: S&P 500 logs strong May gain as tech stocks lead rally
data.description:
Wall Street pushed higher on Friday as traders kept a close watch on any updates related to the U.S.-Iran conflict.

The blue-chip Dow was up 0.72%, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) added 0.22%, and the tech-focused Nasdaq Composite gained 0.2%.

On a month-to-date basis, the benchmark S&P 500 gained nearly 6.4% in May, driven by strong big tech earnings as well as renewed optimism among investors about a potential Iran-U.S. ceasefire deal.

However, on a sector-by-sector basis, three of the 11 S&P sectors closed in the green in May, while the remaining eight were in the red.

Seeking Alpha analyst Agar Capital highlighted that the S&P 500 posted a 6.4% monthly gain, but it was primarily driven by the technology sector, which surged 19.79%.

“Eight of the eleven sectors posted negative returns. Therefore, we can see that an extremely small group of stocks is driving the index higher. As such, I do not believe this is a healthy market environment, and I would expect volatile selling pressure if some form of bad news hits the marketplace.”

The analyst also noted that the ceasefire appears to be holding for now, but it remains fragile. Investors are pricing in a solution to the Iran crisis, but they have not yet fully priced the risk of another collapse in negotiations.

Technology (XLK) and Healthcare (XLV) were the strongest performers in May, with gains of 19.26% and 2.69%, respectively.

“Concentration continues to be one of the market's main challenges. Positioning in large-cap technology stocks currently sits at the 95th percentile historically, according to Bloomberg estimates. If everyone is already invested in the same themes, it becomes difficult to sustain current stock prices, and the margin for disappointment regarding future earnings in the second half of the year is virtually zero,” the analyst added.

At the other end of the spectrum, Energy (XLE) and Utilities (XLU) were the biggest detractors in May, with losses of 5.38% and 5.3%, respectively.

Sector-wise monthly performance breakdown:

  • Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLK): +19.79%
  • Health Care Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLV): +2.40%
  • Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY): +2.18%
  • Materials Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLB): -0.56%
  • Communication Services Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLC): -0.68%
  • Industrial Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLI): -0.87%
  • Real Estate Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLRE): -0.88%
  • Financial Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLF): -1.07%
  • Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLP): -1.58%
  • Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLU): -4.35%
  • Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLE): -5.6%

Top 10 S&P 500 gainers in May:

The S&P 500’s May leaders were overwhelmingly concentrated in AI infrastructure and memory-related names, highlighting a narrow, cycle-driven rally led by semiconductors, storage, and data center plays.

On the individual front, Dell (DELL) was the biggest winner in May, gaining more than 104% following its blowout quarterly results and buoyant outlook, followed by Micron Technology (MU) with an 87.40% gain and Datadog (DDOG) at 84.62%.

Here is the list of the top 10 gainers:

  • Dell (DELL): +104.76%
  • Micron Technology (MU): +87.40%
  • Datadog (DDOG): +84.62%
  • Super Micro Computer (SMCI): +75.11%
  • CrowdStrike (CRWD): +61.56%
  • First Solar (FSLR): +60.95%
  • QUALCOMM (QCOM): +60.91%
  • NetApp (NTAP): +60.41%
  • Fortinet (FTNT): +60.23%
  • Sandisk (SNDK): +59.27%

“What is interesting about May is not the growth in AI hardware stocks, since that is completely predictable, but the software recovery. Just a couple of months before their earnings reports, Datadog, CrowdStrike, and Fortinet had declined anywhere from 25% to 30% as investors were starting to write them off as potential “AI casualties.” However, those same companies reported strong earnings results in May, with each suggesting that AI was expanding their potential customer base rather than eliminating it. In short, investors were wrong and are quickly reversing themselves,” Agar Capital concluded.

data.title: Snap breaks six-session winning streak
data.description:
Snap (SNAP) shares broke a six-session winning streak, as the stock closed 3.5% lower at $5.71 on Friday.

The Snapchat owner gained about 5% in the preceding six sessions. Overall, the stock has fallen over 28% so far this year, compared to the over 10% rise in the broader S&P 500 Index.

SNAP is down 1% over the past one month. The stock closed 0.5% higher on Thursday.

Looking at Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating, SNAP has a Hold rating with a score of 3.27 out of 5. The company received an A+ in the prospect of growth, while it got a D- in the valuation factor.

Turning to the Wall Street community, nine analysts gave SNAP a Buy and above, 31 analysts have given the stock a Hold recommendation, and three recommended Sell or lower.

“Snap remains a sell as North American user declines threaten long-term shareholder value despite recent cost-cutting and profitability focus,” said Seeking Alpha analyst Gary Alexander, adding that SNAP's user base is shifting toward lower-ARPU regions, undermining monetization gains and limiting the upside from improved ad formats and cost discipline.

Seeking Alpha analysts are bullish and see the stock as a Buy.

Earlier in the month, Santa Monica, California-based Snap posted a 12% rise in first-quarter revenue driven by increased spending from advertisers and said that ​its number of daily active users returned to growth.

data.title: Delta Air Lines ends flat after a sharp recent rally
data.description:
Delta Air Lines ended Friday flat at $82.49.

Shares of the airline have rallied 21.74% over the past six trading sessions, sharply outperforming the S&P 500 index, which has gained 2.86% over the same period.

Bullish sentiment around the stock has also been supported by positive analyst commentary. Seeking Alpha analyst Dhierin Bechai recently reiterated a Buy rating on Delta, citing strong financials, resilient premium travel demand, and the company’s loyalty business, while projecting 31% upside to a $91.82 price target despite lowering 2026 EBITDA and free cash flow estimates amid Middle East-related pressures.

Similarly, Seeking Alpha analyst Narek Hovhannisyan maintained a Buy rating on Delta, highlighting the carrier’s strong first-quarter performance, diversified revenue streams, and premium-focused strategy, while noting the stock remains undervalued relative to peers despite expected margin pressure from higher fuel costs.

Still, Seeking Alpha’s Quant Ratings system is more cautious on the shares. Delta currently carries a Hold rating with a score of 3.16 out of 5, supported by an A+ grade for profitability, while receiving a C+ for growth and a C for valuation.

Overall, sentiment remains favorable, with both Seeking Alpha authors and Wall Street analysts maintaining Buy and Strong Buy consensus ratings, respectively, on the stock.

data.title: Devon Energy gets $8B offer for Marcellus shale assets - Reuters
data.description:
Devon Energy (DVN) has received a ~$8B offer from money manager Stone Ridge Asset ‌Management for its Marcellus shale assets in Pennsylvania that previously belonged to Coterra, Reuters reported Friday.

Stone Ridge submitted its Marcellus offer as a way to initiate conversations about a deal with Devon (DVN), according to the report, which also said Devon had not made any decisions on the future of the natural gas-focused position and that there is no guarantee that the Stone Ridge offer would lead to a sale.

The proposal reportedly includes utilizing the largest asset-backed securitization ever in the U.S. oil and gas industry, although the actual size of the financing is not known.

ABS financing has found favor in recent years as a way to fund acquisitions of mature oil and gas assets which typically provide limited growth but offer established production levels and low decline ​rates, making them attractive to financial investors looking for steady returns.

Devon (DVN) management, including CEO Clay Gaspar, has said the company is looking to optimize the business ​following the Coterra merger, which closed earlier this month.

The Marcellus was expected to account for ~​20% of Devon's (DVN) 1.6M boe/day production forecast in 2026, according to a presentation earlier this year.

data.title: The Elmet Group Co. GAAP EPS of -$0.02, revenue of $56M
data.description:
• The Elmet Group Co. press release (ELMT): Q1 GAAP EPS of -$0.02.
  • Revenue of $56M.
  • Gross profit margin improved 260 basis points to 21.2% of revenue compared to 18.6% of revenue Q1 2025.
  • Adjusted EBITDA increased to approximately $9.2 million, or 16.4% of revenue, compared to approximately $4.5 million, or 9.6% of revenue, in Q1 2025.
  • Open order backlog increased to approximately $113.3 million, up from approximately $96.3 million at the end of Q4 2025 and approximately $74.7 million at the end of Q1 2025.

data.title: Castlelake confirms early stage interest in offer for easyJet (update)
data.description:
Update 3:55pm: Adds company comment.

EasyJet (EJTTF) rose 1% in London trading amid takeover speculation. Castlelake confirmed that it's in the early stages of considering a possible offer for the airline.

No approach has been made to the board of easyJet, Castlelake said in a statement on Friday. Under UK rules, Castlelake has to announce by June 26 if it will make a firm intention to make an offer or won't make a bid. 

Earlier on Friday, a Betaville "uncooked" alert said takeover interest for easyJet was coming from a private equity firm working with an aircraft leasing company.
